coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Pb2+ and Hg2+ with two types of carbon dot nanohybrids synthesized from the same biomass
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhaoying
Jin, Wenying
Wang, Fangxiao
Li, Tuanchuan
Nie, Jinfang
Xiao, Wencheng
Zhang, Qing
Zhang, Yun
coredata.dc:description: This work initially describes two ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors that use dual- and three-emission carbon dot (CD) nanohybrids for highly sensitive detection of sub-nanomolar Pb2+ and Hg2+, respectively. The two new types of CD hybrids are prepared via solvothermal treatment of the same extracts of bamboo leaves. This strategy is facile, cost-effective, environmentally-friendly and useful, as it avoids the post-modification of CDs or their mixing with other fluorescent probes which are typically adopted in most of CD-based ratiometric nanosensors previously reported. More importantly, the utilities of the two new nanosensors that enable built-in correction of external effects are well demonstrated by achieving detection limits as low as ˜0.14 and 0.22 nM (3σ) for Pb2+ and Hg2+, respectively. This makes them be among the most sensitive systems currently available for sensing the two analyte ions. The satisfactory recovery results of analyzing a series of real river water examples additionally validate their feasibilities for practical applications. To the best of our knowledge, this may be the first report of applying the same natural biomass as raw material to synthesize two types of multi-emission CD hybrids for ratiometric sensing. Considering their simplicity, low cost, good sensitivity and specificity, and accuracy, the developed ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors hold great potential to find wide applications in environmental monitoring, water safety, and waste management.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heavy metal ions
Ratiometric fluorescent detection
Carbon nanodot
Lead ion
Mercury ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of multi-emission fluorescent CD nanohybrids

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangxi Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Guangxi Medical and Health Key Research Project
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support from the Guangxi Key Research Project (No. GuikeAB17129003 ), the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21765007 , 21565012 , 21874032 , and 21365009 ), and the Guangxi Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars (No. 2018GXNSFFA281002 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of solvothermal synthesis of dual- and three-emission CD nanohybrids using the extracts of bamboo leaves under neutral (without Na2CO3) and alkaline (with Na2CO3) conditions, respectively, for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Pb2+ and Hg2+, respectively.
TEM images of the CD nanohybrids synthesized under (A) neutral and (B) alkaline conditions. Insets are high-resolution TEM images.

(A) UV/Vis spectra of dilute aqueous solutions of the two types of CD hybrid products obtained under the (1) neutral and (2) alkaline conditions. Insets are their photographs under natural light (left) and UV light (right). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra (λ ex = 400 nm) measured from the two CD nanohybrid solutions shown in (A).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra recorded from a dilute aqueous solution of the dual-emission CD nanohybrid before and after its applications for the analysis of a blank sample (i.e., water without the analyte ion) and a 0.4 μM Pb2+ sample. (B) Results of the I 653/I 493 ratios obtained from analysing the blank water sample and different metal ions with the dual-emission CD hybrid sensing system (Pb2+, 0.4 μM; other metal ions, 1 mM). All corresponding fluorescence emission spectra were measured at a single excitation of 400 nm. Each error bar represents a standard deviation across three replicate experiments.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra recorded from a dilute aqueous solution of the three-emission CD nanohybrids before and after its applications for the analysis of a blank sample (i.e., water without the analyte ion) and a 1 μM Hg2+ sample. (B) Results of the I 611/I 491 ratios obtained from analysing the blank water sample and different metal ions with the three-emission CD nanohybrid sensing system (Hg2+, 1 μM; other metal ions, 1 mM). All corresponding fluorescence emission spectra were measured at a single excitation of 400 nm. Each error bar represents a standard deviation across three replicate experiments.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra (λ ex = 400 nm) of the dual-emission CD nanohybrid ratiometric probe upon the exposure to different artificial water samples with varying Pb2+ concentrations (C Pb2+) in a range of 0–1000 nM. (B) A relationship between the I 653/I 493 ratios and the C Pb2+ values. Inset shows the calibration curve that describes the linear relationship between the ratios of I 653/I 493 and the logarithm values of C Pb2+ ranging from 0.6 to 800 nM with a regression equation of y = 0.7424 + 0.4359x (R = 0.9826). Each error bar represents a standard deviation across three replicate experiments.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra (λ ex = 400 nm) of the three-emission CD nanohybrid ratiometric probe upon the exposure to different water samples with varying Hg2+ concentrations (C Hg2+) in a range of 0–2000 nM. (B) A relationship between the I 611/I 491 ratios and the C Hg2+ values. The inset shows the calibration curve that describes the linear relationship between the ratios of I 611/I 491 and the C Hg2+ ranging from 1 to 1000 nM with a regression equation of y = 4.624 - 0.00243x (R = 0.9918). Each error bar represents a standard deviation across three replicate experiments.
Comparison of the developed fluorescent Pb2+ sensor with some previous ones.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aLOD, limit of detection; bLCR, linear concentration range.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Pb2+ and Hg2+ with two types of carbon dot nanohybrids synthesized from the same biomass

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: a College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, P.R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: b College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, P.R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work initially describes two ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors that use dual- and three-emission carbon dot (CD) nanohybrids for highly sensitive detection of sub-nanomolar Pb2+ and Hg2+, respectively. The two new types of CD hybrids are prepared via solvothermal treatment of the same extracts of bamboo leaves. This strategy is facile, cost-effective, environmentally-friendly and useful, as it avoids the post-modification of CDs or their mixing with other fluorescent probes which are typically adopted in most of CD-based ratiometric nanosensors previously reported. More importantly, the utilities of the two new nanosensors that enable built-in correction of external effects are well demonstrated by achieving detection limits as low as ˜0.14 and 0.22 nM (3σ) for Pb2+ and Hg2+, respectively. This makes them be among the most sensitive systems currently available for sensing the two analyte ions. The satisfactory recovery results of analyzing a series of real river water examples additionally validate their feasibilities for practical applications. To the best of our knowledge, this may be the first report of applying the same natural biomass as raw material to synthesize two types of multi-emission CD hybrids for ratiometric sensing. Considering their simplicity, low cost, good sensitivity and specificity, and accuracy, the developed ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors hold great potential to find wide applications in environmental monitoring, water safety, and waste management.

ce:section.ce:para: With increasing urbanization and industrialization, the worldwide environmental pollution is getting more and more serious. The pollution of water by heavy metal ions such as Pb2+ and Hg2+ is among the most serious global environmental issues [1–4]. These species show highly toxicological and carcinogenic effects in human body. They are able to form complexes with bioligands containing nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen groups. Such interactions can break hydrogen bonds, change molecular structures of proteins, damage functions of nucleic acids, or inhibit activities of enzymes [5]. In fact, heavy metal ions are considered to belong to the highest toxicity class of pollutants. They are not biodegradable and thus could be biologically accumulated in ecological systems and food chain continually, finally exposing top-level predators to very high levels [6]. For this reason, safe limits or maximum pollution levels have been defined for heavy metal ions in drinking water by different countries and various organizations such as World Health Organization (WHO) and U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [6,7]. For instance, the safe limits of Pb2+ and Hg2+ in drinking water recommended by the WHO are restricted to be 10 μg/L (ca. 48 nM) and 1 μg/L (ca. 5 nM), respectively [6]. Human exposure to an excess amount of Pb2+ impairs a series of neurological functions [8]. The excessive Hg2+ intake may damage various organs and the immune system, or even cause death [9]. Therefore, highly sensitive and specific detection of heavy metal ions at trace levels in water are of great importance for waste management, environmental protection, and water safety.

The routine techniques for the analysis of heavy metal ions are atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [10], atomic emission spectroscopy (AES) [11], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [12], X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XFS) [13] and ion-selective electrodes [14].These conventional methods have their own advantages, but they might still suffer from either cost-intensive instruments, complicated pretreatments and professional skills (i.e., the AAS, AES, ICP-MS and XFS) or low detection sensitivity (i.e., the ion-selective electrodes).

Some efforts have been alternatively dedicated to designing simple yet sensitive fluorescent methods that take advantage of fluorescent dyes [15] or fluorescent nanomaterials (e.g., semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [16], gold [17] or silver [18] nanoclusters, and carbon dots (CDs) [19–24]). Among these reported fluorescent probes, CDs have received significant attention in recent years because they possess many attractive properties [19–24]. Key of such features involves facile preparation, chemical inertness, non-blinking, low photo-bleaching, good biocompatibility and non-toxicity (according to the currently available results of cytotoxic and in vivo toxic assessment [25,26]). As a result, the last several years have witnessed fast progress in the development of a variety of CD-based fluorescent methods for the detection of heavy metal ions such as Pb2+ [20], Hg2+ [21], Cd2+ [22], Cr3+ [23], and Ag+ [24]. These methods are commonly based on the chelation of the ions with functional groups on the CDs, resulting in either quenched (turn-off) or enhanced (turn-on) fluorescence [27].

Two strategies, which make the use of turn-off or turn-on of the fluorescence, are generally designed to realize quantitative analysis by measuring the absolute intensity of a single emission peak or the intensity ratio of two different emission peaks. By comparison, the ratiometric strategy has been proven to allow for a more precise and sensitive measurement because of its built-in correction for light source, sensitivity of instrument, and matrix background [26,28]. A series of ratiometric nanosensors with dual-emission fluorescent systems have been reported for the detection of Cu2+ [26], Hg2+ [29,30], Fe3+ [31], Cd2+ [32], and so on. These systems typically consist of either dye-modified CDs or a mixture of CDs and other fluorescent nanomaterials (i.e., CdSe/ZnS QDs [26] or gold nanoclusters [29,31]) or dyes such as rhodamine B [31]. Nevertheless, the exploration of ratiometric nanosensors with CDs to sensitively, selectively detect heavy metal ions still remains in the early stage. Particularly, developing simpler and more economic multi-emission fluorescent systems that do not require the post-modification of CDs or their mixing with other fluorescent probes could be more desirable.

Herein, for the first time, we describe the proof-of-concept of two complementary ratiometric fluorescent sensors with many of these merits which use two new types of multi-emission CD nanohybrids synthesized from the same natural biomass. The term “hybrids” refers to the nanoprobes made of composite materials that are not prepared from pure chemicals. As depicted in Fig. 1 , both types of nanohybrids are prepared using bamboo leaves as the same raw material. More specifically, after the flavonoids [33] and chlorophyll [34] are extracted from these leaves via ethanol treatment, they further serve as the main starting chemicals for subsequent solvothermal synthesis of dual- and three-emission fluorescent CD nanohybrids under the neutral and alkaline conditions, respectively. The utilities of the resulting multi-emission nanohybrids are well demonstrated with specific ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Pb2+ and Hg2+ in artificial water samples as well as real river water samples. In particular, since the proposed ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors possess a unique advantage of built-in correction of external effects, the amplified detection of the two analyte ions with limits less than 0.23 nM could be achieved, which makes them two of the most sensitive Pb2+ and Hg2+ sensing systems in comparison with many other fluorescent methods recently reported [20,29,30,40–62]. To the best of our knowledge, this may be the first report of using the same natural biomass (bamboo leaf) as starting material to synthesize two new sorts of multi-emission CD nanohybrids which can serve as different ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors.

Bamboo leaves (Sinarundinaria Nakai) were collected on our campus (Guilin, China). Anhydrous ethanol, sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), mercury chloride (HgCl2) and other metal salts were obtained from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). All of the other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Unless otherwise specified, all stock and working solutions were prepared with deionized water with a specific resistivity of 18.2 MΩ•cm that was produced from an ultrapure water system (UPS-II-20 L) of Chengdu Yuechun Technology Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Real environmental water samples were collected from the Lijiang River located in Guilin, China.

UV/Vis absorption measurements were carried out on Cary 50 spectrophotometer (Varian). Fluorescence spectra were measured with F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi). Fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime were determined on FLS1000 steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested by Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using NicoletiS10 spectrometer (Thermo). Micromorphology and size of the CD hybrid products were tested by JEM-2100 F transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of multi-emission fluorescent CD nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: For preparaing the dual-emission CD nanohybrids, cleaned and dried small pieces of bamboo leaves (10 g) were first mixed with 120 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Then, the mixture was heated to 100 °C with gentle stirring and reflux, followed by the temperature maintenance for 24 h. The resultant mixture was stirred continuously until it was cooled to room temperature (25 °C). The supernatant in the mixture was subsequently filtered with a filter membrane (0.22 μm in pore size). After 15 mL of filtrate was mixed with 5 mL of water, the mixture was added into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 mL) and further heated at 180 °C for 5 h. Moreover, the three-emission CD hybrids were prepared under the same experimental conditions except that 5 mL of a Na2CO3 solution in water (0.4 M) was mixed with 15 mL of the filtrate before heating at 180 °C for 6 h in another 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. After the two autoclaves were cooled down to room temperature (25 °C), the supernatant in each autoclave was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane and further dialyzed against water. The resulting CD nanohybrid solutions were finally stored at 4 °C.

In a typical experiment for the Pb2+ detection, 10 μL of dual-emission CD hybrid solution, 490 μL of HEPES buffer (25 mM, pH 7) and 500 μL of an artificial water sample or a river water sample were mixed and incubated at room temperature (25 °C) in dark for 2 h. Then, fluorescence measurement was carried out at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The fluorescence emission intensities at 493 (I 493) and 653 (I 653) nm were recorded. A calibration curve was plotted with the ratio of I 653/I 493 as the ordinate and the Pb2+ concentration (C Pb2+) or its logarithm value (Log C Pb2+) as the abscissa. Moreover, as for the Hg2+ detection, 12.5 μL of three-emission CD hybrid solution, 487.5 μL of HEPES buffer (25 mM, pH 7) and 500 μL of an artificial water sample or a river water sample were mixed well, followed by 2 h incubation at room temperature (25 °C) in dark. Fluorescence measurement was subsequently performed at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm and the intensities of fluorescence emission intensities at 491 (I 491) and 611 (I 611) were recorded. A calibration curve was plotted with the ratio of I 611/I 491 as the ordinate and the Hg2+ concentration (C Hg2+) as the abscissa. Moreover, the comparison (specificity) experiments were performed in the same way but using a blank water sample (i.e., deionized water without Pb2+ and Hg2+) or other thirteen sorts of metal ions (i.e., Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ag+, Na+ and K+) instead of the two target ions.

TEM was first used to characterize the morphology and structure of the as-prepared two types of CD nanohybrids produced under the neutral and alkaline conditions. As seen from their typical TEM images shown in Fig. 2 , both these CD nanohybrids are nearly-spherical, mono-dispersed particles with a diameter range of 3–7 nm. Their higher-resolution TEM images (Fig. 2, insets) further indicate the crystallinity of each sort of CD hybrids with a lattice spacing of ca. 0.22 nm similar to that of the graphite (100) facet [30]. This suggests that every CD hybrid may possess a crystalline graphite-like structure.

Optical characterization of the two types of CD hybrid solutions was then carried out. As represented in Fig. 3 A (inset), each solution shows good aqueous dispersibility and exhibits light yellow and red colour before and after UV excitation, respectively. Their UV/Vis absorption spectra are also displayed in Fig. 3A (curves 1 and 2). Both these nanohybrid solutions have the same absorption peaks at ˜270 nm but different intensities. It has been reported that the CD’s absorption peak recorded at 250–300 nm indicates the typical absorption behaviour of an aromatic π system in nanocarbon [26] and flavonoids [33] that had been extracted from bamboo leaves here. Moreover, the chlorophyll could also be extracted from these leaves [34] and then served as the other main starting chemical for subseuent CD synthesis. Both of a strong absorption peak and a weak absorption peak are thus measured at around 400 nm and 650–675 nm in the spectrum of each type of nanohybrids, which typically correspond to the Soret band and Q-band of the chlorophyll-derived porphyrins [35–37], respectively. However, it should be noted that the nanohybrids obtained from the neutral condition possesses two absorption peaks located at 510 and 675 nm, while the two absorption peaks of the nanohybrids produced under the neutral condition are around 502 and 650 nm. That is, besides the flavonoid-related moieties, both types of CD hybrid may have also been loaded with different porphyrin derivatives that are expected to show varying photoluminescence properties.

Fig. 3B reveals fluorescence spectra of the two sorts of CD nanohybrids obtained at a single excitation of 400 nm. As expected, interestingly, the nanohybrids produced under the neutral condition show two emission bands concentrated at 493 and 653 nm (curve 1), and these hybrids prepared in alkaline condition have three emission bands at 491, 611 and 665 nm (curve 2). Figs. S1 and S2 (Supplementary Material) further shows that the fluorescence emission of the dual- and three-emission CD hybrid is all influenced by excitation from 380 to 420 nm. In a short emission wavelength range of 425–575 nm, a red-shifted emission is observed as the excitation increases and corresponding intensity varies as well. The excitation-dependent red-shifted emission properties should be due to the wide size distribution of these nanohybrids, which is a common phenomenon in other previous CDs [30]. On the other hand, in a long emission wavelength range of 600–700 nm, only the emission intensity of the dual- (653 nm) and three-emission (611 and 655 nm) nanohybrids changes and presents an excitation-independent emission feature. Therefore, it can be speculated that, for the two fluorescence spectra, the emission bands at around 490 nm might be from the intrinsic structures of CDs [19]. And the emission bands at 611, 653 and 665 nm presumably stem from porphyrins that were derived from the extracted chlorophyll, for they are quite similar to the characteristic emission bands of porphyrin derivatives previously reported [36,37]. The excitation spectrum for the emission at either 653 or 611 (or 655) nm indicates a maximum excitation wavelength at 400 nm that is thus used in following experiments. In addition, the measrued fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime of the dual-/three-emission CD nanohybrids are ˜4.7%/˜3.8% and ˜2.73 ns/˜2.95 ns (Figs. S3A and 4A; Supplementary Material), respectively.

FTIR and XPS spectra were tested to obtain more explanations about the difference in the fluorescence of the two types of CD nanohybrids. Their FTIR spectra were shown in Figs. S5A and S6A (Supplementary Material). The unique vibration band between 500 and 800 cm−1 indicates the existence of flavonoid-related moieties [33]. The bands at around 1049 and 1641 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations of C − O and CO, respectively [38]. The stretching mode of C − N leads to the band at around 1384 cm−1 [30]. The absorption bands at around 1469 and 1252 cm−1 are assigned to the vibrations of C − N and C − C in a porphyrin ring [39]. The band at around 2927 cm−1is from the CH groups [40], while the stretching vibrations of OH and NH are located at 3444 cm−1 [40]. Though the FTIR spectra of both types of CD hybrids have similar profiles, significant absorption differences can be clearly observed in the wavenumber range from 500 to 1500 cm−1 (Figs. S5A and S6A). The data implies that these nanohybrids are mainly composed of C, O and N, but they are loaded with different levels of similar functional groups like flavonoid moieties and porphyrins that possess different surface distributions and interactions.

In addition, element composition and surface states of these hybrid products were analyzed by XPS. For either the dual-emission hybrid or the three-emission one, one can find from its full range XPS spectrum that four typical peaks of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s are at around 285, 400, and 532 eV, respectively (Figs. S7A and S8A; Supplementary Material). Their high-resolution C 1s, N 1s and O 1s spectra further confirm the presentence of CC/C=C, C–N, C–O, CO, pyrroliC–N, graphic-N and NH groups (Figs. S7B and S8B). The XPS results are in good agreement with that of FTIR. What is more, different element contents are found in both sorts of multi-emission CD hybrids. The relative atomic percentages of C, N, and O are 90.82, 0.93, and 8.25% for the dual-emission CD nanohybrids, respectively, and 77.91, 5.20, and 16.89% for the three-emission ones. Taken together, the FTIR and XPS characterizations prove the varying level, distribution and interaction of similar functional groups in the two types of nanohybrids. Such different surface states thus result in unique photoluminescence properties of their own.

Next, the working principle of the two ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors was tested by measuring their responses towards blank samples (deionized water) and Pb2+ or Hg2+ samples. As clearly shown in Fig. 4 A, for the dual-emission CD nanohybrid, the change in the fluorescent spectra measured before and after its reaction with a blank sample could be ignored (curves 1 and 2). In the two cases, two small I 653/I 493 ratios of ˜0.52 and ˜0.53 were calculated. On the other hand, the assay of a 0.4 μM Pb2+ sample not only decreased the fluorescence intensity at 493 nm but increased the fluorescence intensity at 653 nm (Fig. 4A, curve 3), resulting in a large ratio of I 653/I 493 up to ˜1.88. Moreover, all of the I 653/I 493 values recorded from the mixing of the dual-emission CD hybrids with each of other metal ions (1 mM each) including Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ag+, Na+ and K+, are lower than ˜0.6 (Fig. 4B). It has been reported the Pb2+ can be specifically bound by flavonoid moieties on the CD-based nanoprobe to quench its fluorescence [33] (at 493 nm here). As shown in the FTIR spectra recorded (Fig. S5; Supplementary Material), only the Pb2+ addition caused a significant reduction in the signal of flavonoid-related vibration band between 500 and 800 cm−1, which also prove the specific binding of Pb2+ by the flavonoid moieties. In addition, the Pb2+ addition led to a decrease from 2.73 to 1.49 ns in the fluorescence lifetime of the nanohybrid (Fig. S3B; Supplementary Material). The lifetime reduction reflects the specific dynamic quenching of fluorescence at 493 nm by the analyte, as no meaningful UV/Vis absorption of all these ions tested was seen in a wavelength range of 230 − 800 nm (Fig. S9; Supplementary Material). The involved fluorescence resonance energy transfer in turn resulted in the increase in the fluorescence at 653 nm that presumably originated from certain porphyrin compound [41] on the dual-emission CD nanohybrids.

In addition, as displayed in Fig. 5 A, there is no meaningful change observed in the fluorescent spectra recorded before and after the mixing of a blank sample with the three-emission CD nanohybrids (curves 1 and 2), thus producing two similar ratios of I 611/I 491, i.e., ˜4.91 and ˜4.89, respectively. As expected, adding 1 μM Hg2+ into the same nanohybrid solution led to a remarkable decrease in the fluorescence at 611 nm, while the fluorescence at 491 and 665 nm hardly changed (Fig. 5A, curve 3). A ratio of I 611/I 491 as small as ˜2.34 was obtained. The effect of some other metal ions (1 mM each) on the I 611/I 491 value of the three-emission nanohybrids was also studied, including Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ag+, Na+ and K+. As shown in Fig. 5B, the measured I 611/I 491 values ranging from ˜4.42 to ˜4.93 are far higher than that obtained from the Hg2+ detection which caused the largest response (˜2.34). It is generally known that Hg2+ can quench the fluorescence of certain porphyrin compounds via its interaction with an electron-rich aromatic ring [36,37] (at 611 nm here). In the FTIR spectrum obtained after the Hg2+ addition (Fig. S6A; Supplementary Material), the characteristic bands at ˜1469 and ˜1252 cm−1 that became weaker or even disappeared directly confirms a formation of Hg2+-porphyrin complex [39]. But no changes were observed in the FTIR spectra of the nanohybrids before and after their reactions with other metal ions (Fig. S6B; Supplementary Material). Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of the three-emission CD hybrid rarely changed after the Hg2+ binding (Fig. S4B; Supplementary Material), indicating a static fluorescence quenching process.

After demonstrating the specific sensing of Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions with the dual- and three-emission CD hybrids, their analytical performances are furhter evaluated. Main experimental factors were studied in detail (Figs. S10-S17; Supplementary Material), including the nanohybrid concentration, reaction pH, temperature and time. Under the optimal conditions, the dual- and three-emission nanohybrids were adopted to detect a series of artificial water samples with varying concentrations of Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions in the presence of thirteen types of interference metal ions mentioned above (1 mM each), respectively. Fig. 6 A represents the fluorescence emission spectra obtained from the dual-emission CD nanohybrids used for the Pb2+ detection. One can find that the increase in the Pb2+ level resulted in both of the decrease in the fluorescence at 493 nm and the increase in the fluorescence at 653 nm. Fig. 6B displays the resultant relationship between the I 653/I 493 ratios and Pb2+ concentrations (C Pb2+) tested. As the C Pb2+ increases from 0 to 1000 nM, the values of I 653/I 493 increase from 0.52 to 2.18, showing clear positive Pb2+ level-dependent ratiometric responses. The corresponding calibration curve as the insert of Fig. 6B revealed that the dual-emission nanosensor could linearly detect the Pb2+ in the artificial water samples in the ion concentrations ranging from 0.6 to 800 nM, with a detection limit estimated to be ˜0.14 nM (3σ).

Moreover, the fluorescence spectra recorded for the Hg2+ analysis using the three-emission CD nanohybrids were presented in Fig. 7 A. It is observed that the fluorescence intensity at 611 nm decreases as the Hg2+ concentration increases, clearly displaying negative ion level-dependent responses. Interestingly, a red shift behavior occurred while the fluorescence decreases, which might be attributed to the formation of a bigger conjugation system after the binding of Hg2+ by the porphyrin compounds on the nanohybrids. A corresponding relationship between the I 611/I 491 ratios and the Hg2+ concentrations (C Hg2+) is displayed in Fig. 7B. The values of I 611/I 491 decrease from 4.85 to 1.95 while the C Hg2+ increases from 0 to 2000 nM. The calibration curve in the inset of Fig. 7B additionally suggests that the three-emission ratiometric nanosensor is linearly sensitive to the Hg2+ in artificial water samples in a concentration range of 1–1000 nM. Its detection limit for Hg2+ was estimated to be ˜0.22 nM (3σ).

With these good results in hand, the accuracy and practicability of the two new ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors were assessed by performing recovery tests of Pb2+ and Hg2+ in several reveal Lijiang river water samples according to the same procedures. It should be pointed out that all the river water samples should be filtrated with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove potential interferences before the analysis. Six parallel experiments for each sample were carried out. As shown in Tables S1 and S2 in Supplementary Material, the recovery results of the Pb2+ obtained from the dual-emission CD nanohybrids range from 91.4 to 105.6% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are between 1.4 and 5.7% (n = 6). In the case of three-emission CD nanohybrids for the Hg2+, the ranges of the recovery results and RSDs calculated are 95.3–105% and 4.1–6.9% (n = 6), respectively. Satisfactory recovery percentages and small RSDs should be ascribed to the unique analytical advantage of the two ratiometric nanosensors, namely the built-in correction of experimental effects such as matrix background.

We have successfully developed two new ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors with dual- and three-emission CD nanohybrids for simple, sensitive and specific detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ in complex environmental water such as river water, respectively. The two types of multi-emission nanohybrids were produced through the single-step, cost-effective and green solvothermal treatment of the same natural biomass (i.e., the extracts of the bamboo leaves). This strategy is quite useful, for it avoids the post-modification of CDs or their mixing with other fluorescent nanomaterials or fluorophores (that is typically dopted in many previous ratiometric sensors based on CDs). In particular, sub-nanomolar detection limits were achieved for the Pb2+ (˜0.14 nM) and Hg2+ (˜0.22 nM) ions by using the as-prepared dual- and three-emission CD nanohybrids, respectively, which are far lower than the safe limits of the two heavy metal ions in drinking water recommended by the WHO (Pb2+, 48 nM; Hg2+, 5 nM) [6]. In fact, they may be considered to be among the most sensitive fluorescent sensing systems compared with many recent fluorescent methods for the detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ (Tables 1 and 2
). Because of their simplicity, low cost, built-in correction of external effects, excellent sensitivity and selectivity, and good accuracy, the developed ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors may hold potential applications in a variety of fields such as environmental protection and water safety monitoring.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the Guangxi Key Research Project (No. GuikeAB17129003), the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21765007, 21565012, 21874032, and 21365009), and the Guangxi Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars (No. 2018GXNSFFA281002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.126698.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
